text.title: Health Checks and Auto-Heal: Concepts
text.summary: Three monitoring layers: systemd liveness (process alive via Restart=always), HTTP health endpoints (application responds correctly), auto-heal watcher (application logic is healthy — e.g. Celery processes tasks). Liveness vs readiness: liveness = process responds, readiness = all dependencies accessible.

list.item: Liveness — is the process alive and responding? `systemctl is-active` or `/health/live` (returns 200 with no body)
Readiness — can the service handle requests? `/health` checks all dependencies: DB, Redis, message queue. Returns 200 if all OK, 503 if degraded.

rule.statement: Always return 503 when any critical dependency is unhealthy, not 200 with an error body.
rule.rationale: Load balancers and health check scripts check HTTP status codes, not body content. A 200 with {"status":"error"} will be treated as healthy.

section.title: systemd Watchdog
section.p: Type=notify + WatchdogSec=30s: the application must send WATCHDOG=1 to the NOTIFY_SOCKET every 15s (half the interval). If it stops sending, systemd kills and restarts the process — catching hung states that still pass `is-active`.

rule.statement: Send watchdog notification at WatchdogSec/2 interval, not WatchdogSec. This provides a safety margin before systemd triggers the kill.
rule.rationale: If the notification thread hangs right after sending at exactly WatchdogSec, the next cycle may be too late. Sending at half the interval provides one missed cycle before restart.

section.title: Auto-Heal Watcher
section.p: A long-running watcher process polls all services every 60s. On failure: 5-minute cooldown between restart attempts, max 3 restarts per hour per service. Prevents restart storms.

list.item: Check methods: HTTP /health, systemctl is-active, Celery inspect ping
Restart count decays: subtract 1 per successful check cycle

see-also.link: FastAPI health endpoint, watchdog notify, watcher class
